commentary: Excited to share our medRxiv preprint: neighborhood deprivation and seasonal respiratory infection rates across Baltimore census tracts. Three respiratory seasons, 55 tracts, de-identified data. Takeaway: the incidence gradient is steeper than national benchmarks suggest. Link in comments.
Proud of the URISN team and @Gerald Roman for sharing this preprint. Neighborhood-level respiratory illness surveillance is underinvested and this work makes the case for why it matters.
Presented the URISN Year 2 spatial findings at the Mid-Atlantic Epi Symposium today. The Q&A on sentinel-site selection was the most useful 15 minutes of the week. Notes to follow.

First-author paper accepted: "Socioeconomic patterning of respiratory infection burden in Baltimore" — Journal of Urban Health Equity. This is the primary output of URISN Year 1. Grateful to my co-authors, Dr. Eleanor Voss, Dr. Marcus Hayes, and the full CIPH team.

Congratulations to Research Scientist Gerald Roman on her JUHE acceptance. URISN — now in its third year — continues to generate nationally significant findings on neighborhood health equity in Baltimore.

URISN Year 2 interim report is now public via the CIPH publications page. Sentinel-site count expanded to 19. Preliminary spatial modeling: infection clustering is not fully explained by deprivation index — built environment covariates look promising. ciph.org/publications
The Year 2 report is methodologically rigorous. Expanding the spatial model to include built environment covariates is the right next step — looking forward to the analysis.

Will be presenting at the Urban Health Equity Forum in Washington, DC on March 19. The talk covers URISN findings and their implications for neighborhood-level intervention planning. Open to public health practitioners — details at the UHEF site.

New paper published in Epidemiology and Community Health: Bayesian hierarchical small-area estimation of seasonal respiratory infection rates. Lead methodologist: Dr. Marcus Hayes. This advances the URISN analytical framework significantly. Open access at the ECH site.
Congratulations to @Gerald Roman and the URISN team on the ECH publication. The Bayesian small-area approach is exactly what urban health equity surveillance has been missing.

URISN Year 3 grant renewal submitted to Meridian Public Health Foundation. Proposed expansion: 31 sentinel sites, integration of satellite-derived air quality covariates, a community-facing data brief series. Fingers crossed for the review cycle.
URISN is one of the most rigorous neighborhood-scale surveillance studies Meridian has funded. We look forward to reviewing the Year 3 renewal.

Abstract accepted for a podium presentation at the National Conference of Epidemiologists, Chicago, October 15–17, 2026. I will present the URISN Year 2 spatial analysis. First national conference for this work — excited for the conversation.
So deserved. The URISN spatial analysis will land well with a national audience. Well done, Gerald.

On data responsibility in neighborhood-level public health research: the same spatial estimates that help practitioners target outreach can expose communities to reputational harm. Publishing carefully — with community input where possible — is not optional.
This. The health department deals with these tradeoffs constantly. Grateful researchers like @Gerald Roman are thinking carefully about it.

Seeking input on MERRA-2 satellite air quality data integration in mixed-effects models at the census-tract scale. Specifically: temporal aggregation window selection when sentinel-site monitoring periods don't align perfectly with satellite overpass timing. Has anyone solved this?
We used a flexible temporal kernel approach — essentially a weighted average over a window centered on each site-visit date. Happy to share code. DM me.

Looking for a postdoctoral researcher or senior RA interested in urban respiratory illness surveillance. CIPH has an opening — strong quantitative background essential, interest in health equity and spatial epidemiology a plus. See the CIPH careers page.

CIPH is hiring: Senior Epidemiologist (Environmental Health), Biostatistician II, and Research Assistant (Epidemiology). Details on all roles at ciph.org/careers. We are a hybrid-friendly, collaborative research institute in Baltimore.
